hero.title: McDonald's – Group Ordering Interaction Design
hero.oneLiner: A Winter Quarter interaction design concept for letting friends build one shared McDonald's order without losing track of who picked what.

context.text: This Northwestern EDI project focused on group ordering: the messy moment where several people want McDonald's together, but one person usually ends up collecting texts, remembering edits, checking totals, and fixing the order. The concept explored how a shared cart could let people join from a link, add their own items, see who added what, review the group order, and split or coordinate checkout without turning the experience into a busy social app.

bullets: Mapped the group-ordering journey from invite, join, browse, add item, review, split, and final confirmation
Designed a shared cart model where each item keeps visible ownership instead of becoming an anonymous list
Created invite and join states so friends could enter the order from a lightweight link or QR flow
Explored review readiness cues so the organizer can tell who is done before checkout
Prototyped group cart, participant, and split-payment states using McDonald's visual language from the slide deck
Kept the interface focused on coordination rather than adding chat, feeds, or social noise

decisions.decision: Shared cart ownership vs one combined item list
decisions.why: Made it clear who added each item, which reduces organizer memory work and makes review faster.
decisions.tradeoff: The cart needs more visual structure than a single-user checkout list.

decisions.decision: Invite link flow vs account-heavy collaboration
decisions.why: Let people join quickly without turning a food order into a full social setup process.
decisions.tradeoff: A lightweight invite model needs clear permissions so people understand what they can edit.

decisions.decision: Review readiness vs instant organizer checkout
decisions.why: Helped the organizer avoid checking out before everyone has confirmed their part of the order.
decisions.tradeoff: Adds a coordination step before payment, but prevents more expensive mistakes.

worked: The shared-cart framing made the concept more specific than a generic ordering redesign.
Assigning items to people clarified responsibility without needing chat or comments.
The invite and review states made the organizer role feel lighter and more controllable.
didnt: The prototype still needs stronger edge-case work around someone leaving, changing an item late, or failing to pay.

outcome.text: The result was a group-ordering concept that turns a messy coordination task into a shared cart with visible ownership, lightweight invites, review readiness, and split-oriented checkout. The case study became a focused exercise in designing collaboration inside a quick-service ordering flow.

reflection.text: The useful lesson was that group ordering is not mainly a menu problem. It is a coordination problem: who is in, who picked what, who is ready, and who is paying.

skills.label: Interaction Design
skills.description: Designing how the system behaves, guides, and recovers.

skills.label: Shared Cart UX
skills.description: Designing item ownership, review, and checkout for multiple people.

skills.label: Prototype Testing
skills.description: Using flows to evaluate coordination and organizer burden.

skills.label: Service Coordination
skills.description: Balancing quick ordering with group readiness and payment handoffs.
